claimed_contribution: Introduces a three-level individual-level measurement model of the online purchase funnel that jointly models channel consideration, visit choice, and purchase choice with carryover and spillover effects.
Proposes a method to attribute conversion credit to multiple online channels using estimated incremental effects from both converting and nonconverting paths.
Provides field validation by using a paid-search shutdown experiment and shows how the estimates can inform targeting decisions such as when retargeting emails may reduce conversion probability.
context: The study is set in multichannel online marketing for a hospitality firm's website, where customers can arrive via organic search, paid search, referral, direct, email, and display channels. It focuses on customers' repeated touches and visits before website conversion.
design_and_data: The authors build a probabilistic three-stage model (consideration, visit, purchase) with channel-specific carryover and spillover effects at visit and purchase stages, and estimate it with MCMC. Data come from integrated individual-level path records from a hospitality firm, combining DoubleClick, Omniture, referral, and email systems. The main sample tracks 1,997 visitors over 68 days and includes channel-level visits/touches and 1,128 purchases. They also use a field setting where paid search was paused for one week (Nov 3-9, 2011) to validate incremental-effect predictions.
main_findings: There are significant carryover and spillover effects across channels at both the visit and purchase stages, and their magnitudes differ by channel.
Attribution from the proposed model differs sharply from common metrics: last-click materially undervalues email, display, and referral contributions and overstates search contributions.

In the paid-search shutdown period, observed outcomes were broadly consistent with model predictions of incremental effects, and part of lost paid-search conversions was recaptured by other channels, especially organic search.
one_line_summary: Using individual-level online path data and a three-stage model, the paper estimates channel carryover and spillover effects to produce incremental conversion attribution that differs substantially from last-click metrics and is supported by a paid-search field test.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior multichannel and online advertising work by jointly modeling customer- and firm-initiated channels at distinct consideration, visit, and purchase stages for attribution. It moves beyond single-channel studies and beyond within-site conversion models by incorporating pre-website touches and nonconverting paths in estimating incremental channel impact.
research_gap: The paper argues that widely used attribution metrics (last-click, first-click, and simple weighted variants) ignore timing and sequence of prior touches, nonconverting paths, and cross-channel carryover/spillover, leading to biased channel credit. It also states that prior research had not jointly examined these effects across visit and purchase stages to attribute conversions across multiple online channels.
research_question: How can a firm use individual-level multichannel path data to estimate the incremental contribution of each online channel to website conversions? Specifically, can a model that captures channel consideration heterogeneity and carryover/spillover effects at both visit and purchase stages produce better attribution and decision guidance than commonly used metrics?
